title: Analogy & Classification

glossary.definition: The incorrect answer choices designed to trick you.

mental_model.intuition: Build a bridge sentence that only one answer can cross.
mental_model.description: Analogies aren't about vague associations; they are about precise structural relationships. Formulating the relationship into an exact sentence forces the right answer to stand out and eliminates distractors.

method: Identify the exact logical relationship between the given pair (e.g., cause/effect, part/whole, tool/worker, synonym/antonym).
Formulate this relationship into a short, precise test sentence. For example, 'A is the primary tool used by a B'.
Test each answer choice by plugging its words into the exact same test sentence.
If multiple answers fit the sentence, narrow the relationship by adding more specificity (e.g., 'A is the hand-held tool used by a B').
For classification (odd one out), identify a specific grouping rule that affirmatively applies to all options except one. Don't just pick the one that 'looks different'.

steps: Analyze the given pair: Author and Book.
Create a test sentence: 'An Author is someone who creates a Book'.
Look at the first word of the target pair: Choreographer.
Plug it into the test sentence: 'A Choreographer is someone who creates a [X]'.
Evaluate what a choreographer creates. A choreographer designs and creates dances or ballets.

common_mistakes.title: Relying on 'vibes' instead of sentences
common_mistakes.explanation: Picking an answer because the words 'feel' related, rather than verifying they share the exact same logical bridge.

common_mistakes.title: Reversing the order
common_mistakes.explanation: If the original pair is 'Worker : Tool', selecting an answer choice that is 'Tool : Worker' is incorrect. The order must match exactly.

recall_questions.q: What is the most reliable way to test an analogy relationship?
recall_questions.answer: Formulate a short, precise test sentence using the original pair, and plug the answer choices into that exact sentence.

recall_questions.q: What should you do if multiple answer choices fit your test sentence?
recall_questions.answer: Make the test sentence more specific to narrow down the exact structural relationship.

recall_questions.q: In an odd-one-out question, how do you verify your choice is correct?
recall_questions.answer: Find a rule that affirmatively groups all the OTHER options together, rather than just stating why the chosen option is different.

oa_questions.question: Thermometer : Temperature :: Odometer : ?
oa_questions.approach: Bridge sentence: 'A [Thermometer] is an instrument used to measure [Temperature].' Therefore, 'An [Odometer] is an instrument used to measure [Distance].'

oa_questions.question: Find the odd one out: 1) Copper 2) Iron 3) Brass 4) Zinc
oa_questions.approach: Rule for the others: Copper, Iron, and Zinc are all pure elements on the periodic table. Brass is an alloy.
